metadata.title: Detect Simpson's Paradox with Claude
metadata.note: CLI video: B00 intro · B01 PROBLEM · B02 ASK · B03 CODE · B04 OUTPUT(manim) · B05 CHANGE · B06 slate · B07 slate · B08 slate · B09 outro.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

beats.role_note: the problem, the stakes — BEFORE the CLI
beats.narration_text: Simpson's paradox is the reason observational studies get retracted. You run a clinical trial, pool the data, drug looks great. Then you stratify by gender and every stratum reverses. The pooled result was backwards. This is not a corner case — it happens any time a confounder is unevenly distributed. The question this video answers: how do you detect it and what does Claude do with it when it finds it.
beats.visual_intent: title card: 'pooled says yes, strata say no — Simpson's paradox'; the stakes for clinical trials; no terminal yet

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude. In the terminal: write a Python script that takes a two-by-two-by-two contingency table, computes marginal and stratum-specific effects, flags a Simpson's paradox if signs flip, calls Claude to classify confounder versus mediator, and then computes an adjusted average treatment effect using backdoor stratification.

props.command: claude "Write a Python script: (1) takes a 2x2x2 contingency table; (2) computes marginal and stratum-specific effects; (3) flags Simpson's paradox if signs flip; (4) calls Claude to classify confounder vs mediator; (5) computes adjusted ATE using backdoor stratification"

beats.narration_text: Read it before running it. The adjusted average treatment effect is one line of weighted expectations — the backdoor formula. Sum over strata: expected outcome under treatment times stratum probability, minus expected outcome under control times stratum probability. The confounder is in the denominator of every weight.

props.code:
# simpson_detector.py — Claude Code output

def adjusted_ate(table, strata_probs):
    ATE = sum(
        E(Y|X=1,Z=z)*P(Z=z) - E(Y|X=0,Z=z)*P(Z=z)
        for z, P_z in zip(strata, strata_probs)
    )
    return ATE

# Marginal: 78% vs 72% -> drug looks good
# Stratified men:  60% vs 70% -> drug WORSE
# Stratified women: 40% vs 50% -> drug WORSE
# Simpson's flag: sign flip detected

beats.narration_text: Run it. The top panel shows the pooled data: drug group seventy-eight percent, control group seventy-two. Drug looks good. The bottom panel stratifies by gender. Men: drug sixty, control seventy. Women: drug forty, control fifty. Every stratum reverses. The reversal flag fires. The confounder — gender — is unevenly distributed across treatment arms and that asymmetry flips the pooled result.
beats.visual_intent: Manim: two-panel table showing pooled 78% vs 72% looks good, then stratified men 60/70 and women 40/50 both reverse — REVERSAL annotation in crimson

beats.narration_text: The backdoor stratification gives you the adjusted estimate. Now ask Claude to add a sensitivity check — what if the confounder classification is wrong and the variable is a mediator, not a confounder? Adjusting for a mediator blocks the causal path and introduces a different kind of bias.

props.command:
claude "update simpson_detector.py:
  -> add mediator check: warn if adjusting for mediator
  -> add frontdoor formula as alternative
  -> print both ATE estimates with warnings"

beats.narration_text: The updated script now flags when a candidate adjustment variable is on the causal path — adjusting for it would block the effect you are trying to measure. Two estimates print: the backdoor adjusted ATE and the frontdoor estimate, so you can compare when the mediator path is the only clean route.
beats.visual_intent: slate: show updated output with mediator warning and two ATE estimates printed side by side

beats.narration_text: So Simpson's paradox is not a data error — it is a structural one. The pooled estimate mixes two populations with different baseline rates. Stratification by the confounder breaks that mix. The backdoor formula weights the stratum-specific effects by how often each stratum appears in the population, not the sample.
beats.visual_intent: recap card: 'pooled = mixing populations; stratified = seeing the structure; backdoor = weighting by the world'

beats.narration_text: Your move: take any observational dataset with a treatment and outcome and a candidate confounder. Run this script. If the paradox flag fires, your marginal estimate is wrong and you now have the adjusted one. The next reel covers the back-door criterion — how to prove that your adjustment set is valid.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) run on your own data, 2) check for sign flip, 3) use backdoor ATE; pointer to back-door criterion reel

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI. Then take it apart. At Nik Bear Brown, nikbearbrown dot com.
